title: Seizure Precautions: Keeping the Patient Safe
subtitle: A 24-year-old man with a seizure disorder is admitted after a breakthrough seizure. The nurse sets up bedside seizure precautions and provides safe care during and after a tonic-clonic seizure — protecting the airway and never restraining or placing anything in the mouth.

patient.name: Seizure Precautions
patient.admitDate: 24M with epilepsy admitted after a breakthrough tonic-clonic seizure (missed doses). On seizure precautions for monitoring and medication adjustment.

vitals.temp: Padded rails up, bed low + locked
Side-lying, protect head, do NOT restrain
Nothing in the mouth during a seizure

labs.v: Stay with patient; call for help.

nursesNote: A 24-year-old man with a known seizure disorder is admitted after a breakthrough tonic-clonic seizure following missed medication doses. The nurse places him on seizure precautions: padded side rails kept up, bed in the lowest position with wheels locked, and functioning suction, oxygen, and a bag-valve mask set up at the bedside. The nurse teaches that during a seizure the priority is to stay with the patient, call for help, protect the head, turn the patient to the side to protect the airway, loosen tight clothing, and time the event — while NEVER restraining the limbs or placing anything in the mouth. A seizure lasting longer than 5 minutes is status epilepticus and requires an ordered IV benzodiazepine. Afterward, the nurse keeps the patient side-lying, suctions as needed, reorients, assesses for injury, and documents the onset, duration, characteristics, and postictal state.
providerOrders: Initiate seizure precautions; padded rails up, bed low and locked
Keep suction, oxygen, and a bag-valve mask ready at the bedside
Stay with the patient and protect the airway during any seizure
Administer ordered IV benzodiazepine if a seizure lasts over 5 minutes
Document seizure onset, duration, characteristics, and postictal state

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does setting up seizure precautions involve?

opts.text: Pad the side rails and keep them up
Keep functioning suction and oxygen set up at the bedside
Place a padded tongue blade in the mouth when a seizure starts
Keep the bed in the lowest position with the wheels locked
Have a bag-valve mask available in case of airway compromise
Plan to restrain the patient's arms to stop any movements

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What is appropriate DURING a tonic-clonic seizure?

opts.text: Stay with the patient and call for help
Turn the patient onto their side to protect the airway
Protect the head and clear nearby objects
Force an oral airway or object between the teeth
Time the seizure and note its characteristics
Hold the patient down to stop the convulsions

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order actions when a seizure begins

opts.text: Stay with the patient and call for help
Protect the head and clear nearby objects
Turn the patient to the side and loosen tight clothing
Time the seizure and observe its characteristics
After it ends, keep side-lying, suction PRN, reorient, assess for injury
Padding the side rails and keeping the bed low and locked
Suction, oxygen, and a BVM ready at the bedside
Inserting a bite block or spoon during the seizure
Restraining the limbs to stop the jerking
Side-lying position during and after the seizure
Documenting onset, duration, and movements
Leaving the patient alone to go get help
Giving the ordered benzodiazepine for a seizure lasting over 5 minutes

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence care for a seizure that won't stop
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Protect the airway: side-lying, suction, apply oxygen
Call for help and note the time the seizure started
Ensure IV access and give the ordered IV benzodiazepine
Monitor airway, vital signs, and oxygen saturation continuously
After it stops, provide postictal care and document the event
Keeping suction, oxygen, and a BVM ready at the bedside
Turning the patient to the side during a seizure
Staying with the patient and timing the seizure
Forcing a padded tongue blade between clenched teeth
Restraining the patient's extremities during convulsions
Giving the ordered benzodiazepine for a seizure lasting over 5 minutes
Leaving the bedside to find a tongue blade as the seizure begins
Documenting onset, duration, characteristics, and postictal state

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
